papergraph.title: Real-Time FPGA-RTDS Co-Simulator for Power Systems
papergraph.citation: Conghuan Yang et al. (2018) Real-Time FPGA-RTDS Co-Simulator for Power Systems doi:10.1109/ACCESS.2018.2862893
papergraph.abstract: This paper proposes a co-simulation platform using field-programmable gate array (FPGA) and real-time digital simulator (RTDS) for the simulation of large power systems. It combines the advantages of high computational power from FPGA and better modelling flexibility from RTDS together. The FPGA therefore acts as an efficient and economical extension to RTDS especially when simulating large ac systems. One of the significant advantages of the proposed co-simulator is that it avoids the potential interface error existing in the conventional approach of interfacing transient stability program with electromagnetic transient programs. Two key aspects of the proposed co-simulator are discussed: 1) the interface design between FPGA and RTDS and 2) the hardware implementation and expandability of the platform. Two case studies are presented to verify the simulation accuracy and capability of the proposed co-simulator. The first case simulates a two-area four-machine power system with one area simulated in FPGA and the other area in RTDS. Comparisons are made with the case where the complete system is simulated in RTDS. The second case simulates a system of 141 buses in FPGA to demonstrate the simulator’s capability in simulating large power systems.

combines high computational power of FPGA with modelling flexibility of RTDS
enables efficient and economical extension of RTDS for large AC systems
improvements: interface design between FPGA and RTDS
hardware implementation and expandability of the platform
papergraph.benchmark: simulation accuracy | capability to simulate large AC systems
papergraph.contribution: A real-time co-simulation platform integrating FPGA and RTDS that avoids interface errors present in conventional transient stability–electromagnetic transient program coupling while enabling scalable simulation of large power systems.

papergraph.title: Feedback Control of a Boost Converter Using a NARX-NN-Based Digital Twin Instance: Multi-Domain Analysis and Comparison
papergraph.citation: Karol Najdek et al. (2026) Feedback Control of a Boost Converter Using a NARX-NN-Based Digital Twin Instance: Multi-Domain Analysis and Comparison doi:10.1109/ACCESS.2026.3669779
papergraph.abstract: This paper proposes a data-driven feedback control framework for regulating the output voltage of a boost converter using a Nonlinear AutoRegressive model with eXogenous inputs Neural Network (NARX-NN). The NARX-NN operates as a Digital Twin Instance (DTI) that estimates the output capacitor voltage in real-time, reducing reliance on direct voltage sensing under noisy or faulty measurement conditions. Robustness is validated in closed-loop operation under sensing degradations. The DTI is embedded within the closed-loop structure, where its output serves as a surrogate feedback signal. A systematic, information-theoretic design procedure integrates Mutual Information (MI) analysis for optimal data-sampling frequency selection, correlation and multicollinearity analysis for informative input identification, and False Nearest Neighbours (FNN) for signal-specific delay embedding. These steps ensure that the trained NARX-NN represents the converter’s nonlinear, time-varying dynamics. The case-study circuit with input and output filters exhibits nonlinear and non-minimum-phase behaviour, making it a benchmark for power-electronic control. Frequency Response Analysis (FRA) characterises dynamics across multiple load conditions. Closed-loop stability and robustness are evaluated using the $\mathfrak {D}$ -decomposition providing intuitive gain-selection criteria and stability boundaries in ( $K_{P},K_{I}$ ) space. Three feedback strategies are compared: 1) conventional measurement-based; 2) DTI-based; and 3) DTI-based and look-up-table (LUT) compensator. Simulation results confirm that the DTI-enhanced controller maintains accurate voltage regulation under dynamic conditions, achieving steady-state deviations below ±3% and robust transient performance while keeping computational effort within real-time constraints (sub-GFLOPS). The proposed framework establishes a unified foundation for integrating neural estimators into power-electronic control and advancing adaptive Digital Twin (DT) architectures for next-generation energy-conversion systems.

papergraph.title: Design and Implementation of Scalable Communication Interfaces for Reliable and Stable Real-time Co-Simulation of Power Systems
papergraph.citation: Qi Xiao et al. (2025) Design and Implementation of Scalable Communication Interfaces for Reliable and Stable Real-time Co-Simulation of Power Systems doi:10.1109/PESGM52009.2025.11225688
papergraph.venue: IEEE Power & Energy Society General Meeting
papergraph.abstract: Co-simulation offers an integrated approach for modeling the large-scale integration of inverter-based resources (IBRs) into transmission and distribution grids. This paper presents a scalable communication interface design and implementation to enable reliable and stable real-time co-simulation of power systems with high IBR penetration. The communication interface is categorized into two types: local and remote. In local scenarios, where subsystems are connected within a single local area network (LAN), low-latency communication facilitates the seamless integration of electromagnetic transient (EMT) and phasor-domain models, enabling efficient interactions with power and energy management algorithms. For remote scenarios, data exchange is achieved via internet-based file sharing or VPN-enabled communication. The performance of both methods is evaluated using OPAL-RT as a real-time simulator, demonstrating scalability, effectiveness, and challenges specific to real-time co-simulation applications. To mitigate instability arising from data resolution mismatches in time-sensitive co-simulations, a real-time data extrapolation method is proposed. This approach significantly enhances stability and reliability, ensuring more accurate simulation outcomes. The implementation code is available on GitHub, providing researchers the tools to replicate and expand upon this work.

papergraph.title: Electromagnetic Transient Simulations for Large-Scale Power Grids: A Utility Perspective
papergraph.citation: Hossein Hooshyar et al. (2026) Electromagnetic Transient Simulations for Large-Scale Power Grids: A Utility Perspective doi:10.1109/MPE.2025.3638277
papergraph.abstract: As the global power grid transforms, utilities face growing complexity in modeling, stability assessment, and operational planning. The increasing penetration of inverter-based resources (IBRs), power-electronics-interfaced devices (PEIDs), and distributed energy technologies exceeds the limits of traditional phasor-domain tools, which struggle to capture fast, nonlinear, and unbalanced behaviors. Electromagnetic transient (EMT) simulations have become essential, offering sub-cycle accuracy, detailed switching behavior, and multi-domain integration. EMT is now integrated into planning workflows, interconnection studies, protection coordination schemes, real-time platforms, and digital twin architectures, helping utilities, system operators, manufacturers, and research institutions address modern grid challenges at both device and system levels. This article provides a utility-focused overview of EMT simulation strategies for large-scale power grids, with a particular emphasis on practical implementation, model development and validation, hybrid co-simulation architectures, and digital twin integration. Drawing from real-world experience and evolving industry standards, the article also highlights use cases across the power system lifecycle from planning and protection to procurement and interconnection. Furthermore, it offers a roadmap for scaling EMT practices in support of the future grid reliability and performance.

papergraph.title: Real-Time Surrogate Modeling for Fast Transient Prediction in Inverter-Based Microgrids Using CNN and LightGBM
papergraph.citation: Osasumwen Cedric Ogiesoba-Eguakun et al. (2026) Real-Time Surrogate Modeling for Fast Transient Prediction in Inverter-Based Microgrids Using CNN and LightGBM doi:10.48550/arXiv.2603.29255
papergraph.abstract: Real-time monitoring of inverter-based microgrids is essential for stability, fault response, and operational decision-making. However, electromagnetic transient (EMT) simulations, required to capture fast inverter dynamics, are computationally intensive and unsuitable for real-time applications. This paper presents a data-driven surrogate modeling framework for fast prediction of microgrid behavior using convolutional neural networks (CNN) and Light Gradient Boosting Machine (LightGBM). The models are trained on a high-fidelity EMT digital twin dataset of a microgrid with ten distributed generators under eleven operating and disturbance scenarios, including faults, noise, and communication delays. A sliding-window method is applied to predict important system variables, including voltage magnitude, frequency, total active power, and voltage dip. The results show that model performance changes depending on the type of variable being predicted. The CNN demonstrates high accuracy for time-dependent signals such as voltage, with an $R^2$ value of 0.84, whereas LightGBM shows better performance for structured and disturbance-related variables, achieving an $R^2$ of 0.999 for frequency and 0.75 for voltage dip. A combined CNN+LightGBM model delivers stable performance across all variables. Beyond accuracy, the surrogate models also provide major improvements in computational efficiency. LightGBM achieves more than $1000\times$ speedup and runs faster than real time, while the hybrid model achieves over $500\times$ speedup with near real-time performance. These findings show that data-driven surrogate models can effectively represent microgrid dynamics. They also support real-time and faster-than-real-time predictions. As a result, they are well-suited for applications such as monitoring, fault analysis, and control in inverter-based power systems.

papergraph.contribution: A data-driven surrogate modeling framework enabling fast, accurate, and real-time or faster-than-real-time prediction of electromagnetic transients in inverter-based microgrids using CNN and LightGBM.

papergraph.title: Digital Real-Time Power System Co-simulation via Distributed Transmission Line Model
papergraph.citation: Luca Barbierato et al. (2023) Digital Real-Time Power System Co-simulation via Distributed Transmission Line Model doi:10.1109/EEEIC/ICPSEurope57605.2023.10194853
papergraph.venue: 2023 IEEE International Conference on Environment and Electrical Engineering and 2023 IEEE Industrial and Commercial Power Systems Europe (EEEIC / I&CPS Europe)
papergraph.abstract: The intrinsic complexity of smart grids requires computer-aided power system analysis to evaluate novel monitoring and control strategies and innovative devices. Due to the enormous computational requirements and the necessary Hardware-In-the-Loop (HIL) and Power Hardware-In-the-Loop (PHIL) applications, real-time power system simulation plays a fundamental role in this context. However, performing real-time simulations in a monolithic way, i.e. exploiting a single Digital Real-Time Simulator (DRTS) rack, could result in the inability to create reliable and accurate digital twins of increasingly complex power systems such as smart grids. This paper proposes a digital real-time power system co-simulation to link different DRTS and scale up the viable Power System Under Test (PSUT). It exploits Aurora 8B/10B to manage the data exchange and a Distributed Transmission Line Model (DTLM) to split the PSUT into the two real-time simulation environments. Furthermore, the DTLM permits the absorption of the communication latency, which normally occurs in real-time co-simulation, into the propagation model of a transmission line. With the presented setup, a time step duration of $50 \mu \mathrm{s}$ proves to be stable and accurate when running a co-simulated Electro-Magnetic Transients (EMT) analysis of a power grid scenario by interconnecting two commercial DRTS (i.e. OPAL-RT) with comparable results compared to the monolithic simulation, extending the scalability of future real-time smart grid simulations.

papergraph.title: Digital Twin Framework for Voltage Control and Power Quality Optimization in Single-Stage Grid-Connected Solar PV System
papergraph.citation: Prateek Shrivastava et al. (2025) Digital Twin Framework for Voltage Control and Power Quality Optimization in Single-Stage Grid-Connected Solar PV System doi:10.1109/ICPEE65973.2025.11411561
papergraph.venue: 2025 International Conference on Power Electronics and Energy (ICPEE)
papergraph.abstract: Single-stage grid-connected photovoltaic (PV) systems face significant challenges in maintaining voltage stability and meeting accurate power quality (PQ) standards during rapidly fluctuating irradiance, load variations, and grid disturbances. This study presents a Digital Twin (DT) architecture that enables real-time voltage regulation and PQ optimization by digitally replicating the physical PV system for predictive analysis, controller tuning, and operational decision-making. The proposed framework integrates a highly accurate physical model, analytical computations, and adaptive control algorithms driven by data to minimize total harmonic distortion (THD), maintain voltage within allowable limits, and comply with IEEE 1547 and IEC 61727 standards. The DT synchronizes real-time data from voltage and current sensors to capture dynamic system behavior, allowing for pre-deployment testing of voltage regulation strategies, including classical, adaptive, and AI-assisted methods. The architecture incorporates enhanced filtering and stability assessment techniques based on classical control and predictive & nonlinear control techniques, ensuring robustness under weak grid conditions. Simulation and hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) evaluations demonstrate that the DT-assisted control framework reduces THD by more than 40%, maintains voltage within ± 1 %, and improves transient settling times compared to conventional control methods. Future work will explore the integration of DTs with distributed energy resource (DER) coordination, cyberphysical security mechanisms, and self-learning controllers to enable fully autonomous, grid-supportive PV operations in nextgeneration smart grids.

papergraph.title: A Comprehensive Review on Power System Risk-Based Transient Stability
papergraph.citation: Umair Shahzad (2022) A Comprehensive Review on Power System Risk-Based Transient Stability arXiv:2206.05113v1
papergraph.abstract: Power systems are getting more complex than ever and are consequently operating close to their limit of stability. Moreover, with the increasing demand of renewable wind generation, and the requirement to maintain a secure power system, the importance of transient stability cannot be overestimated. Considering its significance in power system security, it is important to suggest a different methodology for enhancing the transient stability, considering uncertainties. Current deterministic industry practices of transient stability assessment ignore the probabilistic nature of variables (fault type, fault location, fault clearing time, etc.). These approaches typically provide a cautious principle and can result in high-priced expansion projects or operational limits. With the increasing system uncertainties and widespread electricity market deregulation, there is a strong inevitability to incorporate risk in the traditional transient stability analysis. Accurate assessment of transient stability in a modern power network is becoming a strict requirement both in planning and in real-time operation, due to the increasingly intricate dynamics of a power system. Further, increasing sources of uncertainty in forecast state and in the reaction to faults highly implies the implementation of risk-based approach in assessing transient stability. Thus, this paper aims to provide a comprehensive review of risk-based transient stability in power networks and the accompanying research. It is believed that this review can be an inception for researchers in the domain of power system planning and security.

papergraph.title: Real-time co-simulation of adjustable-speed pumped storage hydro for transient stability analysis
papergraph.citation: M. Mohanpurkar et al. (2018) Real-time co-simulation of adjustable-speed pumped storage hydro for transient stability analysis doi:10.1016/J.EPSR.2017.08.010
papergraph.abstract: Abstract Pumped storage hydro (PSH) based generation of electricity is a proven grid level storage technique. A new configuration i.e., adjustable speed PSH (AS-PSH) power plant is modeled and discussed in this paper. Hydrodynamic models are created using partial differential equations and the governor topology adopted from an existing, operational AS-PSH unit. Physics-based simulation of both hydrodynamics and power system dynamics has been studied individually in the past. This paper demonstrates a co-simulation of an AS-PSH unit between penstock hydrodynamics and power system events in a real-time environment. Co-simulation provides an insight into the dynamic and transient operation of AS-PSH connected to a bulk power system network. The two modes of AS-PSH operation presented in this paper are turbine and pump modes. A general philosophy of operating in turbine mode is prevalent in the field when the prices of electricity are high and in the pumping mode when prices are low. However, recently there is renewed interest in operating PSH to also provide ancillary services. A real-time co-simulation at sub-second regime of AS-PSH connected to the IEEE 14 bus test system is performed using digital real-time simulator and the results are discussed.

papergraph.title: Leveraging Real-Time Digital Simulations for Fast Accurate Dynamic and Stability Studies of IBR Dominated Hybrid AC-MTDC Grids
papergraph.citation: Satish Kumar Ancha et al. (2025) Leveraging Real-Time Digital Simulations for Fast Accurate Dynamic and Stability Studies of IBR Dominated Hybrid AC-MTDC Grids doi:10.1109/PESGM52009.2025.11225845
papergraph.venue: IEEE Power & Energy Society General Meeting
papergraph.abstract: In this era of an unprecedented energy transition, the digital representations of dynamic power system models in the time domain for Electro Magnetic Transients including DC (EMTDC) simulations, have become necessary and even mandatory by most grid codes for a range of use cases. This is primarily due to the increased penetration of Inverter Based Resources (IBRs) and other power electronic components like Voltage Source Converters-based High Voltage Direct Current (VSC-HVDC) grids or Multi-Terminal HVDC grids. While accurate, offline EMTDC simulations are limited to some basic, small-scale studies, such as voltage, frequency, fault studies, etc., due to limitations in their performance. In contrast, carefully developed and decoupled models for real-time EMTDC simulations can be both accurate and fast. They can also be leveraged to serve as digital twins/replicas of the existing system to conduct detailed and accurate studies including hardware controllers. In this context, this paper presents a real-time simulation case study of an IBR-dominated, hybrid AC-surrounded Multi-Terminal high voltage DC (AC-MTDC) grid using HYPERSIM on a real-time digital simulator. This paper also includes the integration of Software In-the Loop (SIL) and Hardware In-the Loop (HIL) aspects to show the value of real-time simulations for dynamic and stability studies by considering two different scenarios and by comparing IBR-dominated systems performance with an equivalent synchronous machine (SM)-dominated system.

papergraph.title: Algorithmic Optimal Control of Screw Compressors for Energy-Efficient Operation in Smart Power Systems
papergraph.citation: K. Yelemessov et al. (2025) Algorithmic Optimal Control of Screw Compressors for Energy-Efficient Operation in Smart Power Systems doi:10.3390/a18090583
papergraph.abstract: This work presents the results of a research study focused on the development and evaluation of an algorithmic optimal control framework for energy-efficient operation of screw compressors in smart power systems. The proposed approach is based on the Pontryagin maximum principle (PMP), which enables the synthesis of a mathematically grounded regulator that minimizes the total energy consumption of a nonlinear electromechanical system composed of a screw compressor and a variable-frequency induction motor. Unlike conventional PID controllers, the developed algorithm explicitly incorporates system constraints, nonlinear dynamics, and performance trade-offs into the control law, allowing for improved adaptability and energy-aware operation. Simulation results obtained using MATLAB/Simulink confirm that the PMP-based regulator outperforms classical PID solutions in both transient and steady-state regimes. Experimental tests conducted in accordance with standard energy consumption evaluation methods showed that the proposed PMP-based controller provides a reduction in specific energy consumption of up to 18% under dynamic load conditions compared to a well-tuned basic PID controller, while maintaining high control accuracy, faster settling, and complete suppression of overshoot under external disturbances. The control system demonstrates robustness to parametric uncertainty and load variability, maintaining a statistical pressure error below 0.2%. The regulator’s structure is compatible with real-time execution on industrial programmable logic controllers (PLCs), supporting integration into intelligent automation systems and smart grid infrastructures. The discrete-time PLC implementation of the regulator requires only 103 arithmetic operations per cycle and less than 102 kB of RAM for state, buffers, and logging, making it suitable for mid-range industrial controllers under 2–10 ms task cycles. Fault-tolerance is ensured via range and rate-of-change checks, residual-based plausibility tests, and safe fallbacks (baseline PID or torque-limited speed hold) in case of sensor faults. Furthermore, the proposed approach lays the groundwork for hybrid extensions combining model-based control with AI-driven optimization and learning mechanisms, including reinforcement learning, surrogate modeling, and digital twins. These enhancements open pathways toward predictive, self-adaptive compressor control with embedded energy optimization. The research outcomes contribute to the broader field of algorithmic control in power electronics, offering a scalable and analytically justified alternative to heuristic and empirical tuning approaches commonly used in industry. The results highlight the potential of advanced control algorithms to enhance the efficiency, stability, and intelligence of energy-intensive components within the context of Industry 4.0 and sustainable energy systems.

papergraph.title: Co-simulation of real-time electromagnetic transient and transient stability simulations using dynamic phasor T-Line model
papergraph.citation: H. Konara et al. (2023) Co-simulation of real-time electromagnetic transient and transient stability simulations using dynamic phasor T-Line model doi:10.1016/j.epsr.2023.109611
papergraph.abstract: —This paper presents techniques to interface a Transient Stability Analysis (TSA) based model to an ElectroMagnetic Transient (EMT) based model running on a Real Time Digital Simulator. Practical challenges of implementing such an interface are discussed. The proposed TSA model can be simulated using a substantially larger time-step compared to the EMT model. The interface between the EMT model and the TSA model is implemented using a transmission line modeled using Dynamic Phasors (DP). Time-step delay is the primary cause of numerical instability in co-simulations. In this work, a traveling wave model of a transmission line is used to decouple the EMT and TSA networks. The interface requires that the propagation delay of the interfaced transmission lines be greater than the EMT time-step even though the TSA is simulated using a much larger time-step than the EMT model. The proposed technique is validated using the IEEE 39 bus system and a power system with 500 buses.

papergraph.title: Preventive Control for Power System Transient Stability via Deep Surrogate Models and Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning
papergraph.citation: Kewei Shi et al. (2025) Preventive Control for Power System Transient Stability via Deep Surrogate Models and Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning doi:10.1109/IAECST68792.2025.11414900
papergraph.venue: 2025 7th International Academic Exchange Conference on Science and Technology Innovation (IAECST)
papergraph.abstract: The increasing integration of renewable energy sources has led to reduced system inertia, exacerbating transient stability risks. Traditional preventive control methods, heavily reliant on time-domain simulations, struggle to meet the real-time requirements of modern power grids due to high computational burdens. To address this, this paper proposes a novel preventive control framework that integrates Deep Surrogate Models with Multi-Agent Proximal Policy Optimization. Specifically, deep neural networks are employed to construct a digital twin environment, substituting for slow physical simulations to enable rapid state inference. Furthermore, generators are modeled as cooperative agents, utilizing a refined composite reward function to optimize control strategies. It effectively balances transient stability with thermal constraints under various fault scenarios, offering a robust and real-time decision-support tool for power system security.

papergraph.title: Real-time frequency based reduced order modeling of large power grid
papergraph.citation: Abilash Thakallapelli et al. (2016) Real-time frequency based reduced order modeling of large power grid doi:10.1109/PESGM.2016.7741877
papergraph.venue: IEEE Power & Energy Society General Meeting
papergraph.abstract: Large power systems are complex and real-time modeling of the grid for electromagnetic simulation (EMT) studies is impractical. In general, there are methods that reduce large power system into an equivalent network that requires less computational resource, while preserving electromechanical (low frequency) and high frequency behavior of the original system. This can be achieved by modeling the area not of interest (external area) as a combination of Transient Stability Analysis (TSA) type phasor model equivalent and Frequency Dependent Network Equivalent (FDNE). TSA retains electromechanical behavior, whereas FDNE retains high frequency behavior of the original power system. To this effect, this paper introduces a method of developing FDNE based on an online recursive least squares (RLS) identification algorithm in z-domain, and modeling of reduced power systems as FDNE and as a combination of TSA and FDNE using real-time digital simulators.

keynotes.title: Real-Time FPGA-RTDS Co-Simulator for Power Systems

keynote.title: Real-Time FPGA-RTDS Co-Simulator for Power Systems
keynote.core_problem: Interface errors and computational bottlenecks when coupling transient stability programs with electromagnetic transient programs; inability of standalone RTDS to efficiently simulate large AC power systems in real time.
contributions: A real-time co-simulation platform integrating FPGA and RTDS
Elimination of interface errors between transient stability and electromagnetic transient simulation tools
FPGA as an efficient, economical extension to RTDS for large AC system simulation
Design of FPGA–RTDS interface and hardware implementation supporting expandability
methods: Co-simulation architecture combining FPGA (for high-speed computation) and RTDS (for flexible modeling)
Partitioning of power system models across FPGA and RTDS domains
Hardware-level interface design between FPGA and RTDS
experiments_or_evidence: Case study 1: Two-area four-machine system — one area on FPGA, one on RTDS; results compared against full-system RTDS simulation
Case study 2: 141-bus system simulated entirely on FPGA to demonstrate scalability
assumptions: FPGA can accurately model AC power system components at required real-time resolution
RTDS and FPGA subsystems maintain synchronization and numerical consistency at the interface
Partitioning of system topology preserves dynamic equivalence across domains

gap_signals.text: Conventional interfacing of transient stability and electromagnetic transient programs introduces potential interface errors.
Standalone RTDS struggles with real-time simulation of large AC systems.

important_claims.claim: The proposed co-simulator avoids interface errors present in conventional TSP–EMTP coupling.
important_claims.evidence: Explicitly stated as 'one of the significant advantages' and contrasted with 'conventional approach of interfacing transient stability program with electromagnetic transient programs'.

important_claims.claim: FPGA serves as an efficient and economical extension to RTDS for large AC system simulation.
important_claims.evidence: Directly asserted in abstract: 'The FPGA therefore acts as an efficient and economical extension to RTDS especially when simulating large ac systems.'

important_claims.claim: The co-simulator achieves simulation accuracy comparable to full RTDS simulation.
important_claims.evidence: Case study 1 includes comparisons 'with the case where the complete system is simulated in RTDS', implying validation of accuracy.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Enables modular, scalable real-time power system simulation by decoupling computational load across heterogeneous platforms; provides a template for hardware-in-the-loop co-simulation architectures beyond power systems.

keynotes.title: Feedback Control of a Boost Converter Using a NARX-NN-Based Digital Twin Instance: Multi-Domain Analysis and Comparison

keynote.title: Feedback Control of a Boost Converter Using a NARX-NN-Based Digital Twin Instance: Multi-Domain Analysis and Comparison
keynote.core_problem: Regulating output voltage of a boost converter under noisy or faulty voltage sensing conditions, especially given its nonlinear, non-minimum-phase, and time-varying dynamics.
contributions: A data-driven feedback control framework embedding a NARX-NN as a Digital Twin Instance (DTI) for real-time output capacitor voltage estimation.

A systematic, information-theoretic design procedure integrating Mutual Information (MI) analysis, correlation/multicollinearity analysis, and False Nearest Neighbours (FNN) for input selection and delay embedding.
Multi-domain closed-loop stability and robustness analysis using D-decomposition in (KP, KI) space.
Comparative evaluation of three feedback strategies: conventional measurement-based, DTI-based, and DTI-based with LUT compensator.
Demonstration of sub-GFLOPS computational efficiency enabling real-time implementation.
methods: NARX-NN modeling for real-time output capacitor voltage estimation.
Digital Twin Instance (DTI) embedded in closed-loop feedback as surrogate sensor.
Mutual Information (MI) analysis for optimal data-sampling frequency selection.
Correlation and multicollinearity analysis for informative input identification.
False Nearest Neighbours (FNN) method for signal-specific delay embedding.
Frequency Response Analysis (FRA) across multiple load conditions.
D-decomposition for closed-loop stability boundary characterization in (KP, KI) parameter space.
experiments_or_evidence: Simulation results showing steady-state deviations below ±3% under dynamic conditions.
Robust transient performance validation under sensing degradations (e.g., noise, faults).
Closed-loop stability and robustness evaluation via D-decomposition.
Computational effort verified to remain within real-time constraints (sub-GFLOPS).
assumptions: The boost converter’s dynamics can be adequately captured by a NARX-NN trained on appropriately embedded, informative input signals.
Delay embedding via FNN yields sufficient state reconstruction for the underlying attractor.
Mutual Information reliably identifies optimal sampling frequency for capturing system dynamics without aliasing or redundancy.
D-decomposition provides valid stability boundaries for the DTI-augmented linear(ized) controller design.
limitations: Validation is simulation-only; no hardware or experimental validation reported.
Dependence on accurate offline training data — no online adaptation or retraining mechanism described.
LUT compensator is mentioned but not detailed (e.g., construction method, update policy, or interpolation scheme).
Non-minimum-phase behavior is acknowledged but no explicit handling (e.g., inverse-modeling or feedforward compensation) is described in the control structure.

keynotes.title: Real-Time Surrogate Modeling for Fast Transient Prediction in Inverter-Based Microgrids Using CNN and LightGBM

keynote.title: Real-Time Surrogate Modeling for Fast Transient Prediction in Inverter-Based Microgrids Using CNN and LightGBM
keynote.core_problem: Electromagnetic transient (EMT) simulations of inverter-based microgrids are computationally intensive and unsuitable for real-time monitoring, fault response, and control.
contributions: A data-driven surrogate modeling framework combining CNN and LightGBM for fast transient prediction in inverter-based microgrids.
Demonstration of variable-specific model strengths: CNN excels for time-dependent signals (e.g., voltage), LightGBM for structured/disturbance-related variables (e.g., frequency, voltage dip).
A hybrid CNN+LightGBM model achieving stable performance across all predicted variables.
Quantified computational speedups: LightGBM >1000×, hybrid model >500× — enabling real-time and faster-than-real-time inference.
methods: Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for time-series signal modeling.
Light Gradient Boosting Machine (LightGBM) for structured and disturbance-related variable prediction.
Sliding-window method for temporal sequence prediction.
Training on high-fidelity EMT digital twin dataset covering ten distributed generators and eleven operating/disturbance scenarios.
experiments_or_evidence: Evaluation on voltage magnitude (CNN R² = 0.84), frequency (LightGBM R² = 0.999), voltage dip (LightGBM R² = 0.75), total active power.
Computational speedup measurements: LightGBM >1000×, hybrid >500× vs. EMT simulation.
Performance comparison across variable types and models (CNN, LightGBM, hybrid).
assumptions: High-fidelity EMT digital twin data is representative of real-world inverter-based microgrid dynamics.
Sliding-window temporal structure captures sufficient dependency for accurate short-term transient prediction.
Input features (e.g., disturbance type, generator states) are observable and suitable for surrogate training.

gap_signals.text: Lack of surrogate models enabling both real-time and faster-than-real-time prediction for monitoring, fault analysis, and control.
No single ML model achieves consistently high accuracy across diverse microgrid variables (time-dependent vs. structured/disturbance-related).
EMT simulations are computationally intensive and unsuitable for real-time applications.

datasets_or_materials: High-fidelity EMT digital twin dataset of a microgrid with ten distributed generators.
Eleven operating and disturbance scenarios: faults, noise, communication delays.

important_claims.claim: CNN achieves high accuracy for time-dependent signals such as voltage (R² = 0.84).
important_claims.evidence: Reported R² value for voltage magnitude prediction using CNN.

important_claims.claim: LightGBM shows better performance for structured and disturbance-related variables: R² = 0.999 for frequency and 0.75 for voltage dip.
important_claims.evidence: Reported R² values for frequency and voltage dip using LightGBM.

important_claims.claim: The hybrid CNN+LightGBM model delivers stable performance across all variables.
important_claims.evidence: Stated qualitative result without specific metrics; contrasted against individual model variability.

important_claims.claim: LightGBM achieves >1000× speedup and runs faster than real time; hybrid achieves >500× with near real-time performance.
important_claims.evidence: Explicitly reported speedup factors and runtime characterization relative to EMT simulation.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a validated, variable-aware surrogate modeling strategy and benchmark dataset structure for real-time transient emulation in power electronics–dominated systems; methodology is transferable to other cyber-physical systems requiring multi-timescale or mixed-signal surrogate modeling.

keynotes.title: Digital Real-Time Power System Co-simulation via Distributed Transmission Line Model

keynote.title: Digital Real-Time Power System Co-simulation via Distributed Transmission Line Model
keynote.core_problem: Monolithic real-time simulation using a single Digital Real-Time Simulator (DRTS) rack is insufficient for scalable, accurate digital twins of complex smart grids, especially under Hardware-In-the-Loop (HIL) and Power Hardware-In-the-Loop (PHIL) requirements.
contributions: Proposes a digital real-time power system co-simulation framework linking multiple DRTS units to scale the viable Power System Under Test (PSUT).
Introduces a Distributed Transmission Line Model (DTLM) that partitions the PSUT across simulation environments.
Enables absorption of communication latency into the transmission line propagation model, mitigating co-simulation timing instability.
methods: Use of Aurora 8B/10B protocol for inter-DRTS data exchange.
Distributed Transmission Line Model (DTLM) for spatial and temporal partitioning of the PSUT.
Co-simulated Electro-Magnetic Transients (EMT) analysis across interconnected commercial DRTS platforms (OPAL-RT).
experiments_or_evidence: Stable and accurate co-simulation achieved with 50 μs time step duration.
Comparably accurate results between co-simulated and monolithic EMT simulations on OPAL-RT platforms.
assumptions: Communication latency can be meaningfully embedded within the physical propagation dynamics of transmission lines.
Aurora 8B/10B provides sufficiently deterministic and low-jitter inter-rack connectivity for real-time constraints.
The PSUT topology permits natural partitioning at transmission line boundaries without compromising model fidelity.

gap_signals.text: Monolithic DRTS racks cannot scale to support reliable and accurate digital twins of increasingly complex power systems such as smart grids.
Communication latency in real-time co-simulation typically undermines stability and accuracy—unaddressed in prior monolithic or naively coupled approaches.

important_claims.claim: The DTLM permits absorption of communication latency into the propagation model of a transmission line.
important_claims.evidence: A time step of 50 μs proves stable and accurate in co-simulated EMT analysis, with results comparable to monolithic simulation.

important_claims.claim: The proposed co-simulation extends scalability of future real-time smart grid simulations.
important_claims.evidence: It links multiple commercial DRTS (OPAL-RT) to scale the viable PSUT, enabling larger and more complex real-time digital twins.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a latency-aware modeling pattern (DTLM) and interconnect protocol integration (Aurora) applicable to distributed real-time simulation of other physics-based networked systems requiring strict determinism, e.g., cyber-physical energy systems or multi-domain HIL testbeds.

keynotes.title: Digital Twin Framework for Voltage Control and Power Quality Optimization in Single-Stage Grid-Connected Solar PV System

keynote.title: Digital Twin Framework for Voltage Control and Power Quality Optimization in Single-Stage Grid-Connected Solar PV System
keynote.core_problem: Single-stage grid-connected photovoltaic (PV) systems struggle to maintain voltage stability and meet power quality standards under rapidly fluctuating irradiance, load variations, and grid disturbances.
contributions: A Digital Twin architecture for real-time voltage regulation and power quality optimization in single-stage grid-connected PV systems
Integration of physical modeling, analytical computation, and adaptive/data-driven control algorithms within the DT framework
Enhanced filtering and stability assessment techniques using classical, predictive, and nonlinear control for robustness under weak grid conditions
Pre-deployment testing capability for multiple voltage regulation strategies (classical, adaptive, AI-assisted)
methods: Digital Twin architecture combining high-fidelity physical modeling and real-time sensor synchronization
Adaptive control algorithms driven by data
Classical control, predictive control, and nonlinear control techniques
Enhanced filtering and stability assessment methods
experiments_or_evidence: Simulation evaluations
Hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) evaluations

gap_signals.text: Future work will explore the integration of DTs with distributed energy resource (DER) coordination, cyberphysical security mechanisms, and self-learning controllers

important_claims.claim: The DT-assisted control framework reduces total harmonic distortion (THD) by more than 40%
important_claims.evidence: Simulation and hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) evaluations demonstrate that the DT-assisted control framework reduces THD by more than 40%

important_claims.claim: The framework maintains voltage within ±1 %
important_claims.evidence: Simulation and hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) evaluations demonstrate that the DT-assisted control framework [...] maintains voltage within ± 1 %

important_claims.claim: The framework improves transient settling times compared to conventional control methods
important_claims.evidence: Simulation and hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) evaluations demonstrate that the DT-assisted control framework [...] improves transient settling times compared to conventional control methods

important_claims.claim: The framework ensures compliance with IEEE 1547 and IEC 61727 standards
important_claims.evidence: The proposed framework [...] minimizes total harmonic distortion (THD), maintains voltage within allowable limits, and complies with IEEE 1547 and IEC 61727 standards

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: High — provides a modular DT architecture for voltage and PQ control in single-stage PV systems, with documented performance gains and compatibility with multiple control paradigms (classical, adaptive, AI-assisted); enables pre-deployment strategy validation via simulation and HIL.

keynotes.title: Design and Implementation of Scalable Communication Interfaces for Reliable and Stable Real-time Co-Simulation of Power Systems

keynote.title: Design and Implementation of Scalable Communication Interfaces for Reliable and Stable Real-time Co-Simulation of Power Systems
keynote.core_problem: Instability and unreliability in real-time co-simulation of power systems with high inverter-based resource (IBR) penetration, particularly due to data resolution mismatches and communication latency across subsystems.
contributions: Design and implementation of a scalable communication interface for real-time co-simulation, differentiated into local (LAN-based) and remote (internet/VPN-based) variants
Proposal of a real-time data extrapolation method to mitigate instability from data resolution mismatches
Empirical evaluation of interface performance using OPAL-RT as a real-time simulator
methods: Categorization and implementation of local (LAN) vs. remote (internet/VPN/file-sharing) communication interfaces
Real-time data extrapolation to resolve time-domain resolution mismatches between co-simulated subsystems
experiments_or_evidence: Performance evaluation on OPAL-RT real-time simulator
Demonstration of scalability, effectiveness, and identification of challenges specific to real-time co-simulation
assumptions: Subsystems can be meaningfully partitioned into EMT and phasor-domain models
Local area network infrastructure supports low-latency synchronization required for EMT–phasor co-simulation
Remote communication channels (e.g., VPN) provide sufficient determinism for bounded-latency data exchange in non-critical paths
limitations: No quantitative metrics (e.g., latency bounds, stability thresholds, error rates) reported
Lack of details on extrapolation algorithm (e.g., interpolation order, causality constraints, buffer requirements)
No discussion of security, fault tolerance, or failure recovery in remote communication
No validation on multi-vendor or heterogeneous simulator environments beyond OPAL-RT

gap_signals.text: Lack of details on extrapolation algorithm (e.g., interpolation order, causality constraints, buffer requirements)
No discussion of security, fault tolerance, or failure recovery in remote communication
No quantitative metrics (e.g., latency bounds, stability thresholds, error rates) reported
No validation on multi-vendor or heterogeneous simulator environments beyond OPAL-RT
Need for scalable, reliable communication interfaces tailored to real-time co-simulation with high IBR penetration
Instability arising from data resolution mismatches in time-sensitive co-simulations

important_claims.claim: Real-time data extrapolation significantly enhances stability and reliability in co-simulation.
important_claims.evidence: Claim stated without quantitative evidence; described as enhancing stability and reliability 'ensuring more accurate simulation outcomes' — no metrics or comparative results provided.

important_claims.claim: The proposed communication interface enables seamless integration of EMT and phasor-domain models in local LAN settings.
important_claims.evidence: Supported by low-latency communication facilitating efficient interactions with power and energy management algorithms — no empirical latency or synchronization data given.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Medium-high: Provides an open-source implementation framework and identifies concrete challenges (e.g., resolution mismatch, remote determinism) that are reusable as benchmark problems; however, lack of algorithmic detail and evaluation metrics limits direct reproducibility and quantitative comparison.

keynotes.title: A Comprehensive Review on Power System Risk-Based Transient Stability

keynote.title: A Comprehensive Review on Power System Risk-Based Transient Stability
keynote.core_problem: Current deterministic transient stability assessment methods ignore the probabilistic nature of uncertainties (e.g., fault type, location, clearing time), leading to overly conservative, costly decisions and inadequate security in increasingly complex, renewable-integrated, and deregulated power systems.
contributions: Provides a comprehensive review of risk-based transient stability in power networks
Highlights the necessity and motivation for shifting from deterministic to risk-based transient stability assessment
Serves as an inception or foundation for researchers in power system planning and security

gap_signals.text: Increasing system uncertainties and widespread electricity market deregulation create strong inevitability to incorporate risk in traditional transient stability analysis
Current deterministic industry practices ignore the probabilistic nature of variables (fault type, fault location, fault clearing time, etc.)
Deterministic approaches provide a cautious principle and can result in high-priced expansion projects or operational limits
Accurate assessment of transient stability is becoming a strict requirement both in planning and real-time operation due to increasingly intricate dynamics

important_claims.claim: Transient stability assessment must evolve to account for uncertainty due to renewable integration, system complexity, and market deregulation.
important_claims.evidence: Abstract states: 'With the increasing demand of renewable wind generation... the importance of transient stability cannot be overestimated' and 'there is a strong inevitability to incorporate risk in the traditional transient stability analysis.'

important_claims.claim: Deterministic transient stability practices are insufficient for modern power systems.
important_claims.evidence: Abstract states: 'Current deterministic industry practices... ignore the probabilistic nature of variables... and can result in high-priced expansion projects or operational limits.'

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Serves as a foundational survey identifying key research directions, motivations, and methodological gaps for risk-aware transient stability—useful for framing new work, grant proposals, or literature reviews in power system security and uncertainty quantification.

keynotes.title: Real-time co-simulation of adjustable-speed pumped storage hydro for transient stability analysis

keynote.title: Real-time co-simulation of adjustable-speed pumped storage hydro for transient stability analysis
keynote.core_problem: Lack of integrated, real-time co-simulation frameworks that jointly model penstock hydrodynamics and power system dynamics for adjustable-speed pumped storage hydro (AS-PSH) units during transient events.
contributions: First demonstration of real-time co-simulation of an AS-PSH unit coupling penstock hydrodynamics and power system dynamics.
Modeling of AS-PSH in both turbine and pump operational modes using physics-based hydrodynamic PDEs and an existing operational governor topology.
Integration of AS-PSH into the IEEE 14-bus test system for sub-second transient stability analysis in a digital real-time simulator.
methods: Physics-based hydrodynamic modeling using partial differential equations (PDEs) for penstock dynamics.
Governor topology adopted from an existing, operational AS-PSH unit.
Real-time co-simulation framework linking hydrodynamic and power system models.
Transient stability analysis under turbine and pump modes within a digital real-time simulator.
experiments_or_evidence: Real-time co-simulation at sub-second time scale.
AS-PSH unit connected to IEEE 14-bus test system.
Results discussed for dynamic and transient behavior in both turbine and pump modes.
assumptions: Governor topology from an existing operational AS-PSH unit is representative and transferable.
Hydrodynamic PDE model sufficiently captures relevant penstock dynamics for transient stability purposes.
IEEE 14-bus system provides adequate representation of bulk power system interactions for this study.

gap_signals.text: Physics-based simulation of both hydrodynamics and power system dynamics has been studied individually in the past.
Recently there is renewed interest in operating PSH to also provide ancillary services.

important_claims.claim: Co-simulation provides insight into the dynamic and transient operation of AS-PSH connected to a bulk power system network.
important_claims.evidence: Real-time co-simulation at sub-second regime performed using digital real-time simulator; results discussed for turbine and pump modes.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Enables modular, real-time co-simulation of hydropower components with power system models—valuable for transient stability studies, ancillary service design, and controller validation for next-generation PSH.

keynotes.title: Leveraging Real-Time Digital Simulations for Fast Accurate Dynamic and Stability Studies of IBR Dominated Hybrid AC-MTDC Grids

keynote.title: Leveraging Real-Time Digital Simulations for Fast Accurate Dynamic and Stability Studies of IBR Dominated Hybrid AC-MTDC Grids

contributions: A real-time simulation case study of an IBR-dominated hybrid AC-MTDC grid using HYPERSIM on a real-time digital simulator
Integration of Software-in-the-Loop (SIL) and Hardware-in-the-Loop (HIL) to validate real-time simulation for dynamic and stability analysis
Comparative performance evaluation of IBR-dominated vs. synchronous machine (SM)-dominated systems under two scenarios
methods: Real-time EMTDC simulation using HYPERSIM on a real-time digital simulator
Model decoupling and careful development of real-time-capable dynamic power system models
SIL and HIL integration for co-simulation with hardware controllers
experiments_or_evidence: Case study comparing IBR-dominated and SM-dominated system behavior under two distinct scenarios
Validation of real-time simulation accuracy and speed for dynamic and stability studies
Demonstration of digital twin utility via SIL/HIL integration
assumptions: Real-time decoupled EMTDC models can preserve sufficient accuracy for dynamic and stability studies
HYPERSIM-based real-time platform provides adequate fidelity and timing determinism for IBR and VSC-HVDC modeling
SIL/HIL integration reflects realistic controller behavior in closed-loop operation

keynotes.title: Algorithmic Optimal Control of Screw Compressors for Energy-Efficient Operation in Smart Power Systems

keynotes.title: Preventive Control for Power System Transient Stability via Deep Surrogate Models and Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning

keynote.title: Preventive Control for Power System Transient Stability via Deep Surrogate Models and Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning
keynote.core_problem: Traditional preventive control methods for power system transient stability are computationally too slow for real-time operation due to reliance on time-domain simulations, especially under low-inertia conditions caused by increasing renewable energy integration.
contributions: A novel preventive control framework integrating Deep Surrogate Models and Multi-Agent Proximal Policy Optimization (MAPPO)
A digital twin environment built with deep neural networks to replace slow time-domain simulations for rapid state inference
A cooperative multi-agent formulation where generators act as agents with a refined composite reward function balancing transient stability and thermal constraints
methods: Deep surrogate modeling (deep neural networks) for fast system dynamics approximation
Multi-Agent Proximal Policy Optimization (MAPPO)
Cooperative agent design with generator-level control
Composite reward function incorporating transient stability metrics and thermal limits
experiments_or_evidence: Evaluation under various fault scenarios
Demonstration of real-time decision-support capability
Validation of robustness in balancing transient stability and thermal constraints
assumptions: System states and dynamics can be accurately approximated by deep surrogate models trained on simulation data
Generators can be effectively modeled as cooperative agents with shared objectives
Transient stability and thermal constraints are adequately captured by the composite reward function

gap_signals.text: Traditional preventive control methods... struggle to meet the real-time requirements of modern power grids due to high computational burdens.
The increasing integration of renewable energy sources has led to reduced system inertia, exacerbating transient stability risks.

important_claims.claim: The framework offers a robust and real-time decision-support tool for power system security.
important_claims.evidence: It effectively balances transient stability with thermal constraints under various fault scenarios.

important_claims.claim: Deep neural networks substitute for slow physical simulations to enable rapid state inference.
important_claims.evidence: Deep neural networks are employed to construct a digital twin environment.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a template for coupling surrogate modeling with MARL in safety-critical, physics-constrained cyber-physical systems; methodology transferable to other infrastructure domains requiring real-time stability assurance.

contributions: A method for developing Frequency Dependent Network Equivalent (FDNE) using online recursive least squares (RLS) identification in the z-domain
Integration of FDNE with Transient Stability Analysis (TSA)-type phasor models for hybrid reduced-order modeling
Implementation and demonstration on real-time digital simulators

z-domain RLS identification suffices for capturing frequency-dependent impedance characteristics in real time

Full text excerpt:
Co-simulation offers an integrated approach for modeling the large-scale integration of inverter-based resources (IBRs) into transmission and distribution grids.

In local scenarios, where subsystems are connected within a single local area network (LAN), low-latency communication facilitates the seamless integration of electromagnetic transient (EMT) and phasor-domain models, enabling efficient interactions with power and energy management algorithms.
The performance of both methods is evaluated using OPAL-RT as a real-time simulator, demonstrating scalability, effectiveness, and challenges specific to real-time co-simulation applications.
To mitigate instability arising from data resolution mismatches in time-sensitive co-simulations, a real-time data extrapolation method is proposed.
This approach significantly enhances stability and reliability, ensuring more accurate simulation outcomes.

evidence_type_annotations.claim: instrumental measurement

A data-driven surrogate modeling framework enabling fast, accurate, and real-time or faster-than-real-time prediction of electromagnetic transients in inverter-based microgrids using CNN and LightGBM.
No explicit limitation extracted.
Full text excerpt:
This paper presents a data-driven surrogate modeling framework for fast prediction of microgrid behavior using convolutional neural networks (CNN) and Light Gradient Boosting Machine (LightGBM).
A sliding-window method is applied to predict important system variables, including voltage magnitude, frequency, total active power, and voltage dip.
The results show that model performance changes depending on the type of variable being predicted.

The CNN demonstrates high accuracy for time-dependent signals such as voltage, with an $R^2$ value of 0.84, whereas LightGBM shows better performance for structured and disturbance-related variables, achieving an $R^2$ of 0.999 for frequency and 0.75 for voltage dip.
Beyond accuracy, the surrogate models also provide major improvements in computational efficiency.

Full text excerpt:

The proposed framework integrates a highly accurate physical model, analytical computations, and adaptive control algorithms driven by data to minimize total harmonic distortion (THD), maintain voltage within allowable limits, and comply with IEEE 1547 and IEC 61727 standards.

The DT synchronizes real-time data from voltage and current sensors to capture dynamic system behavior, allowing for pre-deployment testing of voltage regulation strategies, including classical, adaptive, and AI-assisted methods.

evidence_type_annotations.claim: Pontryagin maximum principle (PMP)

A mathematically grounded PMP-based regulator that minimizes total energy consumption of a nonlinear electromechanical system comprising a screw compressor and variable-frequency induction motor, enabling improved adaptability, energy-aware operation, and real-time PLC deployment.
hybrid extensions combining model-based control with AI-driven optimization and learning mechanisms, including reinforcement learning, surrogate modeling, and digital twins
Full text excerpt:

This work presents the results of a research study focused on the development and evaluation of an algorithmic optimal control framework for energy-efficient operation of screw compressors in smart power systems.

The proposed approach is based on the Pontryagin maximum principle (PMP), which enables the synthesis of a mathematically grounded regulator that minimizes the total energy consumption of a nonlinear electromechanical system composed of a screw compressor and a variable-frequency induction motor.

Unlike conventional PID controllers, the developed algorithm explicitly incorporates system constraints, nonlinear dynamics, and performance trade-offs into the control law, allowing for improved adaptability and energy-aware operation.
Simulation results obtained using MATLAB/Simulink confirm that the PMP-based regulator outperforms classical PID solutions in both transient and steady-state regimes.

Experimental tests conducted in accordance with standard energy consumption evaluation methods showed that the proposed PMP-based controller provides a reduction in specific energy consumption of up to 18% under dynamic load conditions compared to a well-tuned basic PID controller, while maintaining...[truncated]

Furthermore, the proposed approach lays the groundwork for hybrid extensions combining model-based control with AI-driven optimization and learning mechanisms, including reinforcement learning, surrogate modeling, and digital twins.

The research outcomes contribute to the broader field of algorithmic control in power electronics, offering a scalable and analytically justified alternative to heuristic and empirical tuning approaches commonly used in industry.

A real-time co-simulation platform integrating FPGA and RTDS that avoids interface errors present in conventional transient stability–electromagnetic transient program coupling while enabling scalable simulation of large power systems.
No explicit limitation extracted.
Full text excerpt:

One of the significant advantages of the proposed co-simulator is that it avoids the potential interface error existing in the conventional approach of interfacing transient stability program with electromagnetic transient programs.
Two case studies are presented to verify the simulation accuracy and capability of the proposed co-simulator.

evidence_type_annotations.claim: machine learning model

A data-driven feedback control framework using a NARX-NN-based Digital Twin Instance to estimate output capacitor voltage in real-time, enabling robust voltage regulation under sensing degradation and establishing a unified foundation for neural estimator integration in power-electronic control.

Full text excerpt: A data-driven feedback control framework using a NARX-NN-based Digital Twin Instance to estimate output capacitor voltage in real-time, enabling robust voltage regulation under sensing degradation and establishing a unified foundation for neural estimator integration in power-elec...[truncated]
Full text excerpt:

This paper proposes a data-driven feedback control framework for regulating the output voltage of a boost converter using a Nonlinear AutoRegressive model with eXogenous inputs Neural Network (NARX-NN).

Simulation results confirm that the DTI-enhanced controller maintains accurate voltage regulation under dynamic conditions, achieving steady-state deviations below ±3% and robust transient performance while keeping computational effort within real-time constraints (sub-GFLOPS).

The proposed framework establishes a unified foundation for integrating neural estimators into power-electronic control and advancing adaptive Digital Twin (DT) architectures for next-generation energy-conversion systems.

evidence_type_annotations.claim: co-simulation offers an integrated approach for modeling

A scalable communication interface design and implementation enabling reliable and stable real-time co-simulation of power systems with high IBR penetration, including a real-time data extrapolation method to mitigate instability from data resolution mismatches.
challenges specific to real-time co-simulation applications
Full text excerpt:
Co-simulation offers an integrated approach for modeling the large-scale integration of inverter-based resources (IBRs) into transmission and distribution grids.

In local scenarios, where subsystems are connected within a single local area network (LAN), low-latency communication facilitates the seamless integration of electromagnetic transient (EMT) and phasor-domain models, enabling efficient interactions with power and energy management algorithms.
The performance of both methods is evaluated using OPAL-RT as a real-time simulator, demonstrating scalability, effectiveness, and challenges specific to real-time co-simulation applications.
To mitigate instability arising from data resolution mismatches in time-sensitive co-simulations, a real-time data extrapolation method is proposed.
This approach significantly enhances stability and reliability, ensuring more accurate simulation outcomes.

A data-driven surrogate modeling framework enabling fast, accurate, and real-time or faster-than-real-time prediction of electromagnetic transients in inverter-based microgrids using CNN and LightGBM.
No explicit limitation extracted.
Full text excerpt:
This paper presents a data-driven surrogate modeling framework for fast prediction of microgrid behavior using convolutional neural networks (CNN) and Light Gradient Boosting Machine (LightGBM).
A sliding-window method is applied to predict important system variables, including voltage magnitude, frequency, total active power, and voltage dip.
The results show that model performance changes depending on the type of variable being predicted.

The CNN demonstrates high accuracy for time-dependent signals such as voltage, with an $R^2$ value of 0.84, whereas LightGBM shows better performance for structured and disturbance-related variables, achieving an $R^2$ of 0.999 for frequency and 0.75 for voltage dip.
Beyond accuracy, the surrogate models also provide major improvements in computational efficiency.

Full text excerpt:

The proposed framework integrates a highly accurate physical model, analytical computations, and adaptive control algorithms driven by data to minimize total harmonic distortion (THD), maintain voltage within allowable limits, and comply with IEEE 1547 and IEC 61727 standards.

The DT synchronizes real-time data from voltage and current sensors to capture dynamic system behavior, allowing for pre-deployment testing of voltage regulation strategies, including classical, adaptive, and AI-assisted methods.

Simulation and hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) evaluations demonstrate that the DT-assisted control framework reduces THD by more than 40%, maintains voltage within ± 1 %, and improves transient settling times compared to conventional control methods.

Future work will explore the integration of DTs with distributed energy resource (DER) coordination, cyberphysical security mechanisms, and self-learning controllers to enable fully autonomous, grid-supportive PV operations in nextgeneration smart grids.

evidence_type_annotations.claim: scalable communication interface design | real-time data extrapolation method
A scalable communication interface design and a real-time data extrapolation method that enhance stability and reliability in real-time co-simulation of power systems with high IBR penetration.
Challenges specific to real-time co-simulation applications, including instability from data resolution mismatches.
Full text excerpt:
Co-simulation offers an integrated approach for modeling the large-scale integration of inverter-based resources (IBRs) into transmission and distribution grids.

In local scenarios, where subsystems are connected within a single local area network (LAN), low-latency communication facilitates the seamless integration of electromagnetic transient (EMT) and phasor-domain models, enabling efficient interactions with power and energy management algorithms.
The performance of both methods is evaluated using OPAL-RT as a real-time simulator, demonstrating scalability, effectiveness, and challenges specific to real-time co-simulation applications.
To mitigate instability arising from data resolution mismatches in time-sensitive co-simulations, a real-time data extrapolation method is proposed.
This approach significantly enhances stability and reliability, ensuring more accurate simulation outcomes.

references: K. Yelemessov et al. (2025) Algorithmic Optimal Control of Screw Compressors for Energy-Efficient Operation in Smart Power Systems doi:10.3390/a18090583

evidence_type_annotations.claim: Pontryagin maximum principle (PMP)

A mathematically grounded PMP-based regulator that minimizes total energy consumption of a nonlinear electromechanical system comprising a screw compressor and variable-frequency induction motor, enabling improved adaptability, energy-aware operation, and real-time PLC deployment.
hybrid extensions combining model-based control with AI-driven optimization and learning mechanisms, including reinforcement learning, surrogate modeling, and digital twins
Full text excerpt:

This work presents the results of a research study focused on the development and evaluation of an algorithmic optimal control framework for energy-efficient operation of screw compressors in smart power systems.

The proposed approach is based on the Pontryagin maximum principle (PMP), which enables the synthesis of a mathematically grounded regulator that minimizes the total energy consumption of a nonlinear electromechanical system composed of a screw compressor and a variable-frequency induction motor.

Unlike conventional PID controllers, the developed algorithm explicitly incorporates system constraints, nonlinear dynamics, and performance trade-offs into the control law, allowing for improved adaptability and energy-aware operation.
Simulation results obtained using MATLAB/Simulink confirm that the PMP-based regulator outperforms classical PID solutions in both transient and steady-state regimes.

Experimental tests conducted in accordance with standard energy consumption evaluation methods showed that the proposed PMP-based controller provides a reduction in specific energy consumption of up to 18% under dynamic load conditions compared to a well-tuned basic PID controller, while maintaining...[truncated]

Furthermore, the proposed approach lays the groundwork for hybrid extensions combining model-based control with AI-driven optimization and learning mechanisms, including reinforcement learning, surrogate modeling, and digital twins.

The research outcomes contribute to the broader field of algorithmic control in power electronics, offering a scalable and analytically justified alternative to heuristic and empirical tuning approaches commonly used in industry.
